title: Project Overview
description: Start with the README to understand why Stencil exists, the Office formats it supports, and the architectural constraint of keeping format-specific XML work behind one stable rendering API. It also maps the Python, CLI, and optional HTTP entry points that the rest of this tour follows.

title: Public Rendering Contract
description: With the project goals established, inspect the package exports and central dispatcher that form the supported Python interface. The dispatcher validates template and output combinations, selects the DOCX, XLSX, or PPTX engine, and funnels failures through a shared exception hierarchy, so these files define the contract every adapter and engine must honor.
languageLesson: Python packages often use __init__.py as a deliberate facade: re-exported symbols keep the public API stable even when implementation modules move. A py.typed marker tells type checkers that the installed package ships usable inline annotations under PEP 561.

title: Command-Line Adapter
description: The Typer CLI adapts the rendering contract from Step 2 for shell users by loading JSON data, invoking the same dispatcher, and translating domain failures into readable command errors. The package manifest connects that module to the installed stencil command while also defining dependencies, build metadata, and quality-tool configuration.
languageLesson: A Python console-script entry point separates command discovery from implementation: the package manager installs an executable that imports and calls the configured Python object. This lets the CLI remain a thin adapter over reusable library code.

title: DOCX Rendering
description: The first format engine implements the dispatch contract by combining template validation with docxtpl-backed WordprocessingML rendering. The invoice and styled-report fixtures show why this layer matters: Jinja variables, conditions, repeated rows, and existing document formatting must survive as the engine produces serialized DOCX bytes.
languageLesson: DOCX files are ZIP packages containing related XML parts. Template engines must preserve WordprocessingML structure and styles while replacing content, because visible text may be split across multiple XML runs.

title: XLSX Rendering
description: Building on the same dispatcher boundary, the spreadsheet engine evaluates Jinja expressions in cells and expands loop blocks into new rows. Its distinguishing responsibility is preserving styles while translating copied formulas, which keeps generated workbooks both visually consistent and computationally correct.
languageLesson: Spreadsheet formulas use relative and absolute references, so copying a rendered row requires translating formulas rather than duplicating their text verbatim. XLSX is also an XML package, but cell styles and formulas live in coordinated workbook parts.

title: PPTX Rendering
description: The presentation engine applies the shared rendering model to a more structural task: editing Open Packaging Convention parts, rendering text across XML runs, and cloning marker slides for repeated data. The status-deck fixture and its JSON payload make the relationship concrete by pairing project placeholders with repeatable milestone slides.
languageLesson: Cloning a PPTX slide requires more than copying its XML: presentation ordering, relationship IDs, and content-type declarations must remain consistent across the package. Run-aware text rendering is necessary because PowerPoint may split one visible placeholder across several XML elements.

title: PDF Conversion Boundary
description: After any Office engine returns document bytes, the dispatcher can hand them to the PDF abstraction. The LibreOffice-backed implementation isolates subprocess execution, time bounds, retries, and detailed failure reporting, while the example script demonstrates the complete Python flow from JSON data through DOCX rendering to a final PDF.
languageLesson: A subprocess boundary should treat timeouts, exit status, temporary files, and retries as explicit failure modes. Wrapping those details behind an abstraction keeps the rendering API deterministic even though conversion depends on an external office suite.

title: Optional HTTP Service
description: The FastAPI module exposes the Step 2 contract to trusted internal callers without moving rendering logic into the web layer. It adds health and metrics endpoints, confines template lookup to an allowed root, and records in-process telemetry; its dedicated tests verify both successful responses and the security and error boundaries.
languageLesson: FastAPI adapters benefit from keeping validation and transport concerns at the boundary while delegating domain work to ordinary Python functions. Resolving a requested path against an approved root is an important defense against directory traversal.

title: Executable Examples
description: The examples guide turns the engine concepts from Steps 4-7 into reproducible authoring and rendering workflows. Sample JSON payloads and template-generator scripts reveal the exact data shapes and marker conventions expected by DOCX and PPTX templates, making this the best place to experiment before creating production templates.

title: Integration Verification
description: Once the examples establish expected behavior, the main integration suite shows how the project protects it across DOCX, XLSX, PPTX, and PDF. Read these tests as an executable specification for loops, formatting preservation, concurrency, and LibreOffice retry behavior, especially when changing a format engine or the dispatcher.
languageLesson: Pytest fixtures and parameterized cases are useful for expressing one behavioral contract across multiple formats. Integration tests at package boundaries catch coordination failures that isolated unit tests of XML helpers or subprocess wrappers may miss.

title: Release and Maintenance
description: Finish with the automation that turns verified code into a published package. The workflow validates and builds distributions, derives release notes from the changelog, manages the GitHub release, and publishes to PyPI, while Dependabot and the operational guide document dependency upkeep and recovery procedures.
languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows use event triggers, jobs, and ordered steps to encode release gates. Keeping package validation, artifact building, release creation, and publishing as distinct stages makes failures easier to diagnose and reduces the risk of publishing an unverified distribution.
